myths.myth: EVMs can be hacked remotely via Bluetooth
myths.rebuttal: EVMs are standalone machines with no wireless communication capabilities (no WiFi, no Bluetooth, no RF receivers). The software is permanently burned into read-only memory chips.

myths.myth: NRIs can vote online in Indian elections
myths.rebuttal: NRIs cannot vote online. While they can register as overseas electors, they must be physically present at their designated polling booth in India to cast their vote.

myths.myth: If I am not on the voter list, I can show my Aadhaar card and vote
triggerPhrases: not on list but have aadhaar
myths.rebuttal: Having an Aadhaar card is not enough to vote. Your name MUST be registered in the electoral roll of your constituency. The Aadhaar card can only be used as a proof of identity at the booth if your name is already on the voter list.
NOTA cannot 'win' an election. Even if NOTA receives the highest number of votes, the candidate with the next highest number of votes is declared the winner.

myths.myth: You can vote multiple times if you have ink removed
myths.rebuttal: Double voting is a criminal offence. The indelible ink used is designed to last for weeks and cannot be easily removed. Additionally, polling officers cross-check your name on the electoral roll.

myths.myth: Government can announce new schemes during elections
myths.rebuttal: Once the Model Code of Conduct (MCC) is in effect, the ruling government cannot announce any new financial grants, lay foundation stones for new projects, or promise new schemes that could influence voters.

myths.myth: You need your physical Voter ID to vote
myths.rebuttal: While the Voter ID (EPIC) is preferred, if your name is on the electoral roll, you can vote using any of the 12 other accepted identity documents, such as an Aadhaar card, PAN card, or Driving Licence.

myths.myth: VVPAT slips can be taken home
myths.rebuttal: VVPAT slips cannot be taken home. The slip is visible for about 7 seconds through a transparent window before it automatically cuts and falls into a sealed drop box.

myths.myth: EVM source code is proprietary and hidden
myths.rebuttal: The EVM source code is independently reviewed by a Technical Expert Committee of eminent professors and is maintained under strict security. It is not open source for security reasons, but it is deeply audited.

myths.myth: Only the Prime Minister appoints the Election Commission
myths.rebuttal: As per recent legislation, Election Commissioners are appointed by the President on the recommendation of a selection committee consisting of the Prime Minister, a Union Cabinet Minister, and the Leader of the Opposition in the Lok Sabha.
myths.source: Chief Election Commissioner and Other Election Commissioners Act, 2023
